sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient, a 38-year-old male, presents with a 3-month history of intermittent cough, initially noticed during physical activity such as loading horses, which has persisted despite a recent cold.

The patient has a history of bronchitis diagnosed by their primary care physician, but a chest X-ray and subsequent CT scan revealed a solitary 2-centimeter nodule in the right upper lobe, leading to a referral to our office.
The patient is a non-smoker, having quit 7 years ago, and has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, for which they take methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, for which they take methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly, and a history of smoking, having quit 7 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient denies pain, shortness of breath, or any other symptoms.
Current medications: Methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure is 124/76, heart rate is 70, respiration rate is 16, oxygen saturation is 98% on room air, and the patient is afebrile.
Physical exam: The lungs are clear and equal bilaterally, with no clicks, rubs, or murmurs on auscultation.
The patient has a little bit of swelling on the knuckles of both hands, which is consistent with rheumatoid arthritis, but pulses are intact and capillary refill is brisk.
Test Results: The patient had a chest CT scan showing a solitary 2-centimeter nodule in the right upper lobe, described as smooth in appearance, with no evidence of emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule, for which further evaluation is recommended.
Problem list: Lung nodule; Rheumatoid arthritis; [status: active].
Plan:
I will order a pulmonary function test (PFT) to establish a baseline for lung function.
I will also schedule a PET CT to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.

The patient will undergo a video-assisted thoracoscopy to remove the nodule, which will involve three small incisions on the side of the right chest, general anesthesia, and a hospital stay of one night.
The patient will have a chest tube in place until the following day.
I will continue to follow up with their rheumatologist and continue their medication therapy as previously outlined.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in a few minutes to sign paperwork and begin the treatment process.
